cyst_kidney: Distinguished by well-defined, fluid-filled lesions with thin walls, unlike the solid appearance of tumors or stones.
Kidney cysts do not enhance with contrast and lack calcification, setting them apart from tumors and stones.
This class is characterized by non-invasive, non-calcified fluid collections within the kidney.
Unlike kidney stones, cysts are hypodense and do not appear as bright, radiopaque spots.
Cystic lesions are easily separated from normal kidneys by the presence of round, low-density areas.
kidney_stone: Identified by hyperdense, calcified foci within the kidney, unlike the fluid-filled cysts or soft tissue tumors.
Kidney stones appear as bright, white spots on CT, distinct from the uniform density of normal kidneys.
This class is set apart by the presence of radiopaque calculi, not seen in cysts or tumors.
Unlike tumors, stones do not enhance or distort the renal contour.
Kidney stones are easily distinguished from all other classes by their high radiodensity and sharp borders.
kidney_tumor: Characterized by a solid, enhancing mass with irregular borders, unlike the smooth, fluid-filled cysts.
Kidney tumors often distort the normal renal contour, setting them apart from stones and normal kidneys.
This class may show heterogeneous enhancement, necrosis, or calcification, features not seen in cysts or stones.
Unlike normal kidneys, tumors are space-occupying lesions with abnormal tissue density.
Kidney tumors are easily separated from cysts and stones by their solid, invasive nature.
normal_kidney: Shows normal size, shape, and parenchymal structure with no evidence of cysts, stones, or tumors.
Normal kidneys have smooth contours and uniform enhancement, unlike the abnormalities seen in other classes.
This class is free of fluid-filled lesions, calcifications, or space-occupying masses.
Normal kidney CT scans serve as a baseline for comparison with all pathological findings.
The absence of abnormal growths or radiodense foci distinguishes normal kidneys from all other classes.
